file_name: Kaushik et al. - 2025 - A deep learning and regression-based system for skin lesion severity assessment.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning and regression-based ensemble system for skin lesion severity assessment. Specifically, it employs ResNet50 (a CNN) for feature extraction from dermatoscopic images in the HAM10000 dataset, followed by XGBoost and Ridge Regression models to predict lesion severity. The results (R2=0.94, MAE=0.13, RMSE=0.20) were obtained using this AI/ML methodology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a deep learning and regression-based ensemble system for skin lesion severity assessment. It details a specific methodology (ResNet50, XGBoost, Ridge Regression) applied to the HAM10000 dataset, reports original quantitative results (R2=0.94, MAE=0.13, RMSE=0.20), and includes sections on methodology, experimental results, and discussion. Although published in conference proceedings (IMACSI 2025), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Deep Learning and Regression-Based System for Skin Lesion Severity Assessment
journal: Proceedings of the 1st International Conference on Intelligent Methods and Advanced Computer Scientific Innovations (IMACSI 2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion severity assessment by developing a deep learning and regression-based ensemble system using dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology employs ResNet50 for feature extraction, which is then integrated with XGBoost and Ridge Regression models via a weighted averaging ensemble strategy. The system was evaluated on an 80/20 train-test split using standard regression metrics including R2 score, MAE, MSE, and RMSE. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed ensemble model significantly outperformed standalone deep learning and classical regressors. The ensemble achieved an R2 score of 0.94, a Mean Absolute Error of 0.13, and a Root Mean Squared Error of 0.20. These findings confirm the effectiveness of hybrid modeling strategies for accurate and robust skin cancer severity prediction.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Pigmented skin lesions (specifically melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevi)
paper_terms: seven categories of pigmented skin lesions
lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses the HAM10000 dataset which comprises images representing seven categories of pigmented skin lesions, including melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevi. The task is lesion severity assessment (regression) rather than multi-class classification, but these are the clinical entities present in the dataset used for the experiment.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin lesion severity prediction' and 'severity assessment' as a regression problem. The model predicts a continuous numeric outcome (severity score) using ResNet50, XGBoost, and Ridge Regression, evaluated with regression metrics (R2, MAE, RMSE, MSE) rather than classification metrics.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images representing seven categories of pigmented skin lesions, including melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevi. The images were obtained from various clinical sources and are available on Kaggle.
classes_used: seven categories of pigmented skin lesions

use.training: The dataset was partitioned into 80% for training (used for model training with 5-fold cross-validation) and 20% for testing.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 5-fold cross-validation process to optimize hyperparameters and monitor early stopping.
use.testing: The remaining 20% of the dataset was used for final model evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure generalization during model training and optimize the hyperparameter alpha for the ensemble model.
To terminate training if validation loss did not improve after 10 epochs, preventing overfitting.
To evaluate the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variables.
To evaluate the average magnitude of errors in the predictions.
To evaluate the average of the squares of the errors.
To evaluate the standard deviation of the prediction errors.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It compared model performance using regression metrics (R2, MAE, RMSE, MSE) and stated that the ensemble model 'significantly outperformed' individual models based on these metric values, but did not provide statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Regression task for skin lesion severity assessment
HAM10000.notes: The ensemble model combining ResNet50 feature extraction with XGBoost and Ridge Regression achieved the highest R2 score (0.94) and lowest error metrics (MAE=0.13, RMSE=0.20). The paper notes that the ensemble model significantly outperformed standalone deep learning models and classical regressors. Note: There is a discrepancy in the text where the abstract and Section 3.8 mention MAE=0.13/RMSE=0.20 for the ensemble, while Section 4.1 text mentions MAE=0.17/RMSE=0.23, but Table 1 consistently lists MAE=0.13 and RMSE=0.20 for the Ensemble model.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble system combining ResNet50 (CNN) for feature extraction with XGBoost and Ridge Regression for severity prediction.
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 was pre-trained (implied by 'pre-trained deep convolutional neural networks') and then fine-tuned on the HAM10000 dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (images resized to match CNN input requirements).

architecture_details.feature_extractor: ResNet50 with Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer to transform output feature maps into fixed-length vectors.
architecture_details.ensemble_strategy: Weighted averaging of XGBoost and Ridge Regression outputs: y_final = α * y_XGBoost + (1 - α) * y_Ridge, where α is a hyperparameter optimized via cross-validation.

training_details.regularization: Early stopping (terminated if validation loss did not improve after 10 epochs)

classification_or_output_head.ensemble_output: Weighted average of XGBoost and Ridge Regression predictions

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels to match CNN input requirements
Min-Max normalization to scale pixel values between 0 and 1
Image augmentation including random rotations, flipping, zooming, and brightness alterations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance generalization and reduce overfitting. Specific probability or range not reported.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid ensemble system, combining ResNet50 for feature extraction with XGBoost and Ridge Regression, significantly outperformed standalone deep learning models (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2) and classical regressors (GBR, XGBoost) in skin lesion severity assessment.
The ensemble model achieved superior predictive accuracy with an R2 score of 0.94, a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.13, and a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 0.20 on the HAM10000 dataset.

The study confirms the effectiveness of hybrid modeling strategies that integrate deep feature representations with classical machine learning regressors for robust and accurate skin cancer severity prediction.

Among standalone models, EfficientNetB0 slightly outperformed ResNet50 and MobileNetV2, while classical regressors trained on extracted features (particularly XGBoost) demonstrated better performance than deep learning regressors alone.
The study acknowledges limitations including reliance on a single dataset (HAM10000) and potential domain bias due to dataset-specific characteristics.

Future work should focus on incorporating larger and more diverse datasets, integrating clinical metadata and patient history, exploring attention mechanisms for lesion localization, and implementing explainable AI (XAI) techniques like SHAP or Grad-CAM to enhance transparency and trust.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which the authors acknowledge introduces potential domain bias due to dataset-specific characteristics and limits generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

There is a notable discrepancy in the reported results for the ensemble model: the Abstract and Section 3.8 claim an MAE of 0.13 and RMSE of 0.20, while Section 4.1 text states an MAE of 0.17 and RMSE of 0.23; however, Table 1 consistently reports MAE=0.13 and RMSE=0.20.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the claim that the ensemble model 'significantly outperforms' individual models; performance comparisons are based solely on metric values.
The regression task uses a severity score derived from the HAM10000 dataset, but the specific clinical definition or source of this continuous severity label is not detailed in the provided context.

The model was evaluated using a single 80/20 train-test split with 5-fold cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning, but there is no mention of external validation on independent datasets to assess robustness.

The study focuses on regression (severity prediction) rather than classification, which is a less common approach in existing literature compared to binary or multi-class classification tasks for skin cancer.
